items.subject: social_psychology
items.topic: the fundamental attribution error in everyday judgments
items.transcript: Imagine you're waiting in line and someone cuts right in front of you. Your first thought is probably, what a rude person. You don't think, maybe they're rushing to a sick relative. This little reaction has a name in social psychology. It's called the fundamental attribution error. The idea is simple. When we explain other people's behavior, we tend to overweight their personality and character, and we underweight the situation they're in. So if a classmate shows up late, we decide they're lazy or careless. We rarely consider that the bus broke down or their alarm failed. Now here's the interesting twist. When we explain our own behavior, we flip it completely. If you show up late, you know it was the traffic, not your character. Researchers have shown this pattern again and again. In one classic study, people watched a speaker read an essay supporting a certain political position. They were clearly told the speaker had been assigned that position by a coin toss. The speaker had no choice at all. Yet the listeners still assumed the speaker personally believed every word. They couldn't shake the feeling that the words revealed the real person inside. So why does this matter? Because it shapes how we judge coworkers, friends, even strangers. It can make us quick to blame and slow to forgive. Once you know about this bias, you can pause and ask a better question. What situation might be driving this behavior? That small habit makes our everyday judgments fairer and a little more generous toward the people around us.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: A bias in how we explain other people's behavior
options.B: Why people decide to cut in line at stores
options.C: How coin tosses are used in political research
options.D: Methods for arriving on time to class each day

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A：整篇讲座的核心是“基本归因错误”，即我们在解释他人行为时过度强调性格、忽视情境。B 错：插队只是开头的引子，属于 too_narrow，不是主题。C 错：抛硬币只是某个实验的细节，属于 surface_word_trap，借用了讲座中的词但不是主旨。D 错：准时与否是举例细节，属于 too_narrow，不是讲座要点。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what do we tend to do when judging others?

options.A: We focus mostly on the situation people are facing
options.B: We overweight personality and underweight the situation
options.C: We judge others exactly the way we judge ourselves
options.D: We refuse to blame anyone for being late at all

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B：教授明确说我们解释他人行为时高估性格、低估情境。A 错：这正好把方向说反了，属于 reversed_logic。C 错：讲座强调我们对自己和他人采用相反的解释方式，属于 wrong_detail（改了关键事实）。D 错：讲座并未说我们拒绝责怪任何人，属于 topic_adjacent，是合理但未提及的内容。

questions.stem: What did listeners assume in the classic essay study?

options.A: That the speaker had chosen the position personally
options.B: That the coin toss was unfair to the speaker
options.C: That the speaker truly believed the assigned position
options.D: That the essay had been written by the listeners

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C：尽管被告知立场是抛硬币随机指定的，听众仍认为演讲者真心相信那些话。A 错：听众其实被告知演讲者没有选择，属于 wrong_detail。B 错：讲座没提硬币是否公平，属于 topic_adjacent。D 错：文章不是听众写的，属于 wrong_detail，与原文事实不符。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about knowing this bias?

options.A: It guarantees we will never misjudge anyone again
options.B: It mainly helps researchers design better experiments
options.C: It proves that personality never affects behavior at all
options.D: It can help us judge others more fairly and kindly

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D：教授结尾说一旦了解这个偏差，就能停下来考虑情境，让判断更公平、更宽容。A 错：把作用夸大成“永不误判”，属于 over_generalization。B 错：把价值局限于研究者，属于 topic_adjacent，讲座强调的是日常判断。C 错：讲座并未否认性格的作用，属于 reversed_logic，把意思反转了。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: how Expressionism represented emotional experience over physical reality
items.transcript: Expressionism is one of the most striking modernist movements of the early twentieth century, and it asks a radical question about what art is for. For centuries, painters tried to capture the world as the eye sees it. Light, proportion, accurate color. Expressionism throws much of that out. Instead of showing how the world looks, Expressionist artists wanted to show how the world feels. The goal was to put inner emotional experience directly onto the canvas, even if that meant distorting physical reality. Think about how you'd draw fear or anxiety. You wouldn't measure anything carefully. You might use jagged lines, harsh colors, a sky that seems to scream. That instinct is exactly what these artists chased. The most famous example is Edvard Munch's painting The Scream. The figure on the bridge has a warped, hollow face. The sky behind is a swirl of blood orange and sick green. None of it is realistic, and that's the point. Munch wasn't recording a sunset. He was recording dread. He once described feeling an infinite scream passing through nature, and the painting tries to make you feel that too. Color and line become emotional tools rather than descriptive ones. This was a real break from tradition. Earlier movements treated distortion as a mistake to be corrected. Expressionism treated it as the whole message. The artist's feeling became more important than the accurate surface of things. And that shift opened a door. Once art could prioritize emotion over appearance, later abstract movements could push even further, eventually leaving recognizable objects behind entirely.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The life and travels of the painter Edvard Munch
options.B: How Expressionism prioritized emotion over physical accuracy
options.C: Techniques for mixing blood orange and green paint
options.D: Why realistic painting dominated the twentieth century

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B：讲座核心是表现主义把情感置于物理真实之上。A 错：蒙克只是举例，聚焦其生平属于 too_narrow。C 错：颜色只是细节，属于 surface_word_trap，借用了画中颜色词。D 错：讲座说写实传统被打破，且二十世纪并非写实主导，属于 wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Expressionist artists want to show?

options.A: The world rendered with accurate light and proportion
options.B: Historical scenes drawn from careful measurement
options.C: How the world feels rather than how it looks
options.D: Calm landscapes painted in soft, gentle colors

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C：教授明确说表现主义要表现世界“感觉如何”，而非“看上去如何”。A 错：准确的光线比例正是被抛弃的旧传统，属于 reversed_logic。B 错：精确测量与表现主义相反，属于 wrong_detail。D 错：讲座举的是刺眼颜色、尖锐线条，并非柔和色彩，属于 topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor say about Munch's painting The Scream?

options.A: It carefully records an actual sunset over a bridge
options.B: It was painted to correct earlier artistic mistakes
options.C: It uses realistic color to calm the viewer down
options.D: Its warped face and swirling sky express dread

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D：教授说画中扭曲的脸和漩涡般的天空表达的是恐惧。A 错：教授特意说蒙克不是在记录日落，属于 reversed_logic。B 错：纠正错误是旧传统的看法，不是这幅画的目的，属于 wrong_detail。C 错：颜色不真实且制造不安，属于 reversed_logic，把效果说反了。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about Expressionism's influence?

options.A: It helped open the way for later abstract movements
options.B: It ended all experimentation in modern painting
options.C: It forced later artists to return to strict realism
options.D: It was admired only by a few painters in Norway

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A：结尾说一旦艺术能把情感置于外表之上，后来的抽象运动就能走得更远。B 错：把影响说成“终结一切实验”，属于 over_generalization。C 错：讲座说趋势是离开写实，而非回归，属于 reversed_logic。D 错：仅限挪威少数画家是没有依据的细节，属于 topic_adjacent。

items.subject: cognitive_psychology
items.topic: the tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon and memory retrieval
items.transcript: You know the feeling. Someone asks for the name of an actor, and it's right there, hovering just out of reach. You can almost taste it. You might even know the first letter, or how many syllables it has, but the word itself won't come. Cognitive psychologists call this the tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon, and it tells us something fascinating about how memory works. Here's the key insight. The phenomenon shows that remembering a word isn't a single all-or-nothing event. Your knowledge of a word is stored in separate pieces. The meaning lives in one place, the sound in another. During a tip-of-the-tongue moment, you've retrieved the meaning and maybe partial sound information, but the full sound pattern stays locked away. So the memory isn't gone. The connection to it is just temporarily weak. Studies back this up nicely. When researchers ask people in this state to guess details, the guesses are surprisingly accurate. People correctly report the first letter far more often than chance, and they often nail the number of syllables. That partial access is the smoking gun. It proves the word is still in there. Older adults tend to experience these moments more frequently, which fits the idea that retrieval connections weaken with age even when knowledge stays intact. There's also a curious effect where similar-sounding wrong words pop up and actually block the target, like a song you can't stop humming. Now, next time I want to look at strategies people use to break the block and finally recover the word.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why older adults gradually lose stored knowledge
options.B: How actors memorize the names in their scripts
options.C: What the tip-of-the-tongue state reveals about memory
options.D: Why songs get stuck and repeat in our heads

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C：讲座围绕舌尖现象揭示记忆如何分块存储与提取。A 错：教授说知识仍在、只是连接变弱，并非丢失知识，属于 wrong_topic。B 错：演员、名字只是引子词，属于 surface_word_trap。D 错：卡住的歌只是一个比喻细节，属于 too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how is knowledge of a word stored?

options.A: Entirely in one place as a single complete unit
options.B: Only as sound, with meaning added much later
options.C: As a memory that disappears once it weakens
options.D: In separate pieces, with meaning and sound apart

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D：教授说词的意义和读音分开存储在不同地方。A 错：与“分块存储”相反，属于 reversed_logic。B 错：意义并非后来才加上，属于 wrong_detail。C 错：教授强调记忆没有消失、只是连接暂时变弱，属于 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What did studies find when people guessed details in this state?

options.A: Their guesses were surprisingly accurate, above chance
options.B: Their guesses were no better than random chance
options.C: They could recall the full word almost immediately
options.D: They guessed the meaning but never the first letter

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A：研究发现人们的猜测出奇准确，首字母正确率远高于随机。B 错：与研究结果相反，属于 reversed_logic。C 错：若能立刻想起整个词就不是舌尖现象，属于 wrong_detail。D 错：人们恰恰常能猜对首字母，属于 reversed_logic，把结果说反了。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss next?

options.A: How meaning and sound are first learned in childhood
options.B: Strategies people use to recover a blocked word
options.C: Why older adults have these moments more often
options.D: How songs become stuck and repeat in the mind

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B：教授结尾明说下次要看人们打破阻塞、找回词语的策略。A 错：童年习得未被预告，属于 wrong_topic。C 错：老年人现象是本讲已讲的内容，不是下一主题，属于 too_narrow。D 错：卡住的歌也是本讲已提到的细节，属于 too_narrow。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: how certain plants use mimicry to attract pollinators
items.transcript: Plants can't move, and they can't chase down a pollinator, so some of them have evolved a remarkable solution. They lie. Certain plants use mimicry to trick animals into pollinating them, and they do it without offering any real reward. We usually picture a fair trade. A bee visits a flower, sips some nectar, and carries pollen along in payment. But mimicry breaks that contract. The plant imitates something the pollinator wants, gets the pollen delivered, and gives nothing back. The most famous case is the bee orchid. Part of its flower has evolved to look and even smell like a female bee. A male bee, fooled by the resemblance, tries to mate with the flower. In the process, pollen sticks to his body. He eventually realizes his mistake and flies off, frustrated, only to be fooled again by the next orchid, carrying the pollen with him. The orchid never produces nectar at all. Now, why mention the false female bee scent? Because the visual trick alone isn't enough. The chemical signal, the imitation pheromone, is what really seals the deception and makes the male commit. This kind of trickery is called sexual deception, and it's surprisingly precise. Each orchid species often mimics just one species of insect. That tight specialization carries a risk, though. If that single pollinator vanishes from a region, the orchid has no backup, and it may fail to reproduce entirely. So this clever strategy is also a fragile one. The very precision that makes the deception work can leave the plant dangerously dependent on one easily fooled partner.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How bees collect nectar from many kinds of flowers
options.B: Why most plants offer a fair reward to pollinators
options.C: How orchids produce unusually large amounts of nectar
options.D: How some plants deceive pollinators through mimicry

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D：讲座核心是某些植物通过拟态欺骗传粉者完成授粉。A 错：采蜜只是用来对比的背景，属于 too_narrow。B 错：公平交易正是被拟态打破的，讲座重点不在此，属于 wrong_topic。C 错：兰花其实根本不产花蜜，属于 surface_word_trap，与原文事实相反。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does the bee orchid offer the bee?

options.A: Nothing in return, since it produces no nectar
options.B: A small but reliable supply of sweet nectar
options.C: Shelter where male bees can safely rest
options.D: Pollen that the bee can eat as food

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A：教授说兰花什么回报都不给，且根本不产花蜜。B 错：与“从不产花蜜”相反，属于 reversed_logic。C 错：讲座没提供庇护所，属于 topic_adjacent。D 错：花粉是被带走授粉，不是给蜜蜂当食物，属于 wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the false female bee scent?

options.A: To prove that bee orchids also produce sweet nectar
options.B: To show the chemical signal is needed to complete the trick
options.C: To describe what a female bee actually smells like
options.D: To argue that vision matters more than smell for bees

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B：教授提到假气味是为了说明仅靠视觉不够，化学信号才让欺骗成功。A 错：兰花不产花蜜，且与气味无关，属于 wrong_purpose。C 错：只是复述气味“像什么”，没解释为何提它，属于 literal_restatement。D 错：讲座说视觉单独不够、需要气味，反而强调气味的关键作用，属于 over_inference。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what risk comes with this strategy?

options.A: The orchid attracts too many different insect species
options.B: The orchid must constantly produce more nectar over time
options.C: Losing its single pollinator can leave the orchid unable to reproduce
options.D: Male bees quickly learn to avoid every orchid forever

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C：教授说每种兰花常只模仿一种昆虫，一旦该传粉者消失，兰花便无备份、可能无法繁殖。A 错：恰恰是高度专一、只针对一种昆虫，属于 reversed_logic。B 错：兰花根本不产花蜜，属于 wrong_detail。D 错：讲座说雄蜂会被下一朵兰花再次欺骗，并非永远回避，属于 reversed_logic。
